summary: Cantrill reflects on the book 'Beautiful Code' and why its subjective title is perfect for a collection with wildly divergent notions of ideal software. He argues that software engineering suffers from being viewed as mundane labor rather than a unique craft at the intersection of mathematics and engineering. The book's value lies not in presenting a unified vision of beauty, but in helping readers develop their own aesthetic sense through exposure to many different approaches.
key_points: Software is unique because 'the blueprints are the thing; the abstraction is the machine'
Programming language choice ultimately affects thought and aesthetic sensibility
The book contains chapters with nearly mutually exclusive definitions of beauty
Reading diverse code helps developers discover and refine their own voice
money_quotes: We suffer -- tremendously -- from a bias from traditional engineering that writing code is like digging a ditch: that it is a mundane activity best left to day labor -- and certainly beneath the Gentleman Engineer.
Software is not like a dam or a superhighway or a power plant: in software, the blueprints _are_ the thing; the abstraction _is_ the machine.
It is long past time that our discipline stood on its feet, stepped out from the shadow of sooty 19th century disciplines, and embraced our unique confluence between mathematics and engineering.
It's hard to imagine how one could have a sense of beauty that is so uncritical as to equally accommodate all of these languages.
You should read this book not because it's cover-to-cover perfection, but rather to hone your own sense of beauty.
key_insight: The subjectivity of 'beautiful code' is a feature, not a bug—engaging with divergent aesthetic visions helps programmers discover and refine their own voice in their craft.
